question: Scenario: Approaching a pedestrian crossing at night
scenario: You are driving at night on a two-lane road. As you approach a pedestrian crossing, you notice a pedestrian on the sidewalk who appears to be about to cross. There are no traffic signals at this crossing.
description: In a scenario, the overall score is based on all sub-question answers.

scenarioQuestions.question: You must slow down and be prepared to stop before the pedestrian crossing.
scenarioQuestions.description: Drivers must slow down and be prepared to stop whenever a pedestrian is present at or near an uncontrolled crossing.

scenarioQuestions.question: It is acceptable to pass through quickly if the pedestrian has not yet stepped off the curb.
scenarioQuestions.description: Even if the pedestrian has not yet stepped into the road, you must slow down and yield if they intend to cross. Passing quickly is dangerous and illegal.

scenarioQuestions.question: You should switch from high beams to low beams when the pedestrian is visible ahead.
scenarioQuestions.description: High beams can blind pedestrians and make it harder for them to judge oncoming traffic. Switch to low beams when a pedestrian is visible.

question: Scenario: Merging onto an expressway
scenario: You are about to enter an expressway via an on-ramp. Traffic on the expressway is travelling at approximately 100 km/h. Your current speed on the ramp is 60 km/h. There is a dashed white line marking the merge zone.
description: In a scenario, the overall score is based on all sub-question answers.

scenarioQuestions.question: You should accelerate on the on-ramp to match the speed of expressway traffic before merging.
scenarioQuestions.description: To merge safely, you must accelerate to match the flow of expressway traffic. Merging at a significantly lower speed creates a hazard for vehicles already on the expressway.

scenarioQuestions.question: Vehicles already on the expressway must yield to vehicles entering from the on-ramp.
scenarioQuestions.description: Merging vehicles must yield to traffic already on the expressway. It is the responsibility of the entering vehicle to find a safe gap and merge smoothly.

scenarioQuestions.question: If there is no safe gap to merge, you should stop at the end of the on-ramp and wait.
scenarioQuestions.description: Stopping on an expressway on-ramp is extremely dangerous. You should slow down to create time to find a gap, but stopping is prohibited except in an emergency.

question: Scenario: Following a large truck on a narrow road
scenario: You are driving behind a large truck on a narrow two-lane mountain road. The truck is travelling at 30 km/h and frequently brakes. Visibility ahead is limited due to the truck's size. A sign indicates a sharp curve 200 m ahead.
description: In a scenario, the overall score is based on all sub-question answers.

scenarioQuestions.question: You should maintain a greater following distance than usual because the truck blocks your view ahead.
scenarioQuestions.description: When following a large vehicle that obstructs your forward view, increase your following distance so you have more time to react to hazards hidden by the truck.

scenarioQuestions.question: It is safe to overtake the truck before the curve if the opposite lane appears clear.
scenarioQuestions.description: Overtaking before a curve is prohibited because you cannot see oncoming traffic. Even if the lane looks clear, a vehicle could appear from around the curve at any moment.

scenarioQuestions.question: When the truck brakes suddenly, your extended following distance gives you additional stopping time.
scenarioQuestions.description: A greater following distance provides more reaction time and stopping distance if the vehicle ahead brakes suddenly, which is especially important on mountain roads.
